header.subtitle: Module 11: Living Memory · Local RAG + Reflect
sec.problema.intro: In session 2 you saw the four memory types. It works. Until the day the project has 200 curated files and the AI loads them all before the first question. Static memory has a ceiling, and it arrives fast.
sec.problema.card.load.title: Static Memory · Load All
sec.problema.card.load.desc: The AI reads every .md at startup. Works for 10 files. At 200, the context is saturated before you type the first question. Every call pays the price of loading what it probably won't use.
sec.problema.card.query.title: RAG Memory · Query on Demand
sec.problema.card.query.desc: The AI doesn't load memory. It queries. AGENTS.md stays at ~500 tokens and says "I'll go fetch it". The rest arrives chunk by chunk, only when relevant. Constant startup. Lean context.
sec.problema.toggle.title: Same scenario, two memory models:
sec.problema.toggle.scenario: Dev asks: "what's the timestamp convention in the project?"
sec.problema.chart.title: Startup tokens as memory grows
sec.problema.chart.caption: Illustrative values. The point isn't the number, it's the curve.
sec.problema.insight.title: P2 taught the what. P11 teaches the how at scale.
sec.problema.insight.desc: It's not "P2 was wrong". The four memory types still hold. What changes is the access strategy: instead of the AI loading everything, it learns to query. And more: it learns to learn, without you rewriting the .md by hand.
sec.rag-fundamentos.intro: RAG means Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The AI doesn't answer from memory. It searches first, then answers. Not magic, a turbocharged library index. Three operations, three concepts. In ninety seconds you have enough to hold a conversation.
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.embed.label: Embed
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.embed.desc: Text becomes a vector. Each text chunk receives coordinates in a many-dimensional space.
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.embed.note: "authentication" and "login" end up as neighbors. "authentication" and "coffee" stay far apart.
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.index.label: Index
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.index.desc: Vector and text go into a database with BM25 (lexical search) and a vector index (semantic search).
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.index.note: Both indexes coexist in the same SQLite. No server, no cloud.
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.search.label: Search
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.search.desc: Question becomes a vector. The database returns the closest chunks. A small LLM reorders by real relevance.
sec.rag-fundamentos.op.search.note: Top-3 or top-5 enter the context. The rest stays out.
sec.rag-fundamentos.pipeline.title: The hybrid pipeline in a diagram
sec.rag-fundamentos.vecspace.title: The vector space in action
sec.rag-fundamentos.vecspace.desc: Each point is an indexed chunk. When the query arrives, the pipeline fires beams looking for the nearest neighbors. The three that light up are the top-K that enters the context.
sec.rag-fundamentos.vecspace.caption: The query "how do I authenticate a user?" doesn't search for the word. It searches for the neighbor. That's why "login", "auth" and "credentials" light up even without appearing in the question's text.
sec.rag-fundamentos.insight.title: Three hits, three responsibilities
sec.rag-fundamentos.insight.desc: BM25 hits terms. Vector hits intent. Rerank hits priority. Alone each one fails on obvious cases. Together they cover most of the real search space. That combination is what separates a decent RAG from a RAG that disappoints you after two weeks.
sec.rag-fundamentos.aside.title: Wait, what is an "embedding" again?
sec.rag-fundamentos.aside.desc: Think of a map where words are points. Words with similar meaning sit close, unrelated words sit far apart. An embedding is the point's coordinate. Models like embeddinggemma turn any text into a coordinate of a few hundred dimensions. You don't need to understand the dimensions, just that distance in space means similarity in meaning.
sec.qmd.intro: QMD is the local search engine built by Tobias Lütke. Runs 100% offline after setup. Hybrid search with BM25, vector and rerank, all inside a single SQLite file. No server, no cloud, no API key. You give it a folder of markdown and it gives you back a queryable index.
sec.qmd.stack.embedding.label: Embedding
sec.qmd.stack.bm25.label: Lexical Search
sec.qmd.stack.vector.label: Vector Search
sec.qmd.stack.rerank.label: Rerank
sec.qmd.storage.title: Where the index lives
sec.qmd.chunking.title: Smart chunking, not dumb cuts
sec.qmd.chunking.desc: QMD doesn't cut text every N characters. It scores boundaries: a heading is worth 50 to 100, a code block 80, a paragraph 20. It targets chunks of ~900 tokens with 15% overlap. For code, it uses tree-sitter (TypeScript, Python, Go, Rust) and respects function boundaries.
sec.qmd.chunking.bad.desc: Cuts mid-sentence. Embedding gets diluted. Retrieval brings back useless fragments.
sec.qmd.chunking.good.desc: Cuts at a heading or end of block. Each chunk stays coherent. The embedding represents a real concept.
sec.qmd.commands.title: The commands you'll actually use:
sec.qmd.tab.setup.note: The first run downloads the models (~300MB embedding + ~600MB reranker). From the second on it's instant.
sec.qmd.tab.search.note: 90% of practical use is qmd query. The other two are for tuning the pipeline or debugging a bad result.
sec.qmd.tab.mcp.note: Through MCP, the agent calls qmd like any other tool, without you writing wrappers. Connects with Claude Code, Cursor, Codex.
sec.qmd.hook.title: QMD is the engine. The fuel is still missing.
sec.qmd.hook.desc: The index is only worth what the memory feeding it is worth. The next section shows how to structure .agents/memory for QMD to chunk well and return useful answers. If memory goes in bad, it comes out bad. Garbage in, garbage retrieved.
sec.memoria-estatica.intro: In session 6 you saw .claude/ as a safe workspace. It works, but it locks knowledge inside Claude. Switching to Cursor or Codex means losing it all. The fix is to pull memory out of the tool silo: it lives in .agents/, which is tool-agnostic. Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, all point to the same place.
sec.memoria-estatica.toolagnostic.title: One memory, many tools
sec.memoria-estatica.tree.title: Anatomy of .agents/memory/
sec.memoria-estatica.format.title: Required format · YAML frontmatter + dense bullets
sec.memoria-estatica.format.desc: QMD chunks these files, so the rule is simple: every bullet has to stand on its own as a searchable fact. Prose is poison. Each token has to carry signal.
sec.memoria-estatica.format.note: Each bullet above fits in a single chunk if needed. Each one answers a different search.
sec.memoria-estatica.prose.bad.title: Prose-heavy (not indexable)
sec.memoria-estatica.prose.bad.note: 42 words to say "always UTC". The chunker cuts mid-sentence, the embedding gets diluted, the search fails.
sec.memoria-estatica.prose.good.title: Dense bullets (indexable)
sec.memoria-estatica.prose.good.note: 17 words, 3 searchable facts. Each one becomes a useful chunk. Each one answers a different question.
sec.memoria-estatica.rule.title: Golden rule · query first, write second
sec.memoria-estatica.rule.desc: Before creating a new file, always run qmd query agent-memory "<topic>". If something already covers the topic, update the existing file. Duplicate memory degrades the rerank: the AI gets stuck between two contradicting chunks and answers both poorly.
sec.limite-estatico.intro: Static memory solves the loading problem. It doesn't solve the capture problem. You still write every .md by hand. And this is where the real ceiling shows up: most of the knowledge your session generates never gets recorded, because you're busy doing the work, not documenting it.
sec.limite-estatico.what.title: What happens in a real session
sec.limite-estatico.what.li.1: You discover that pg-protocol-v1 ignores TZ on insert
sec.limite-estatico.what.li.2: Apply the fix, test, merge, close the ticket
sec.limite-estatico.what.li.3: Decide with the team: refresh tokens always rotate, no grace period
sec.limite-estatico.what.li.4: Do a refactor, realize connection pooling should be global
sec.limite-estatico.what.li.5: End of day: head full, nobody wrote anything in .md
sec.limite-estatico.lost.title: Result in 3 months
sec.limite-estatico.lost.li.1: Another dev falls into the same pg-protocol-v1 bug
sec.limite-estatico.lost.li.2: The decision about refresh tokens becomes "does anyone remember why?"
sec.limite-estatico.lost.li.3: Connection pooling stays local in three different services
sec.limite-estatico.lost.li.4: git log has the truth but nobody's going to read 240 commits
sec.limite-estatico.lost.li.5: The AI has no way to know. You didn't write it down.
sec.limite-estatico.cross.title: And worse: switch tools and lose it all again
sec.limite-estatico.cross.note: Knowledge trapped in a proprietary format is knowledge condemned to become recurring cost.
sec.limite-estatico.chart.title: Manual capture vs automatic capture · by lesson type
sec.limite-estatico.chart.caption: Temporary workaround is low on purpose. Not everything becomes a lesson.
sec.limite-estatico.turn.title: The pivot question
sec.limite-estatico.turn.desc: What if capture was automatic? What if, at the end of every session, someone reconciled the git log, your STATE.md and the conversation, and saved what's worth saving, without you breaking flow? What if the AI did that, but you still decided what becomes a real rule?
sec.reflect.intro: Reflect is a skill (hook to Session 7) that runs at the end of any meaningful session, in any tool. In about two minutes it reads what happened, decides what's worth keeping, proposes candidates and updates memory. You don't break flow. You only review the candidates later.
sec.reflect.step.1.title: Read the session context
sec.reflect.step.2.title: Identify 1 to 3 real lessons
sec.reflect.step.2.desc: rule confirmed by evidence · pattern observed · architectural decision
sec.reflect.step.3.title: Route by confidence
sec.reflect.step.3.desc: high → learn.py (immediate graduate) · medium → stage.py (review queue)
sec.reflect.step.4.title: Write reflection file
sec.reflect.step.5.title: Reindex QMD
sec.reflect.step.5.desc: next session already queries the new knowledge on the first query
sec.reflect.example.title: What a real reflection file looks like:
sec.reflect.example.desc: Example from a real session (anonymized) of a refresh token hotfix. Under 20 lines. No prose. Each bullet is a fact auditable against a commit or test.
sec.reflect.skill.reflect.title: reflect
sec.reflect.skill.reflect.desc: Fires at session end automatically or by command. Uses git log and STATE.md as primary evidence.
sec.reflect.skill.digest.title: conversation-digest
sec.reflect.skill.digest.desc: Fires when you explicitly ask "save what we discussed". Looks at the whole conversation, filters mercilessly, writes only what's worth it.
sec.reflect.hook.title: Reflect proposes. You graduate.
sec.reflect.hook.desc: The automation is in capturing and proposing. Judgment stays human: which lessons become permanent rules, which stay in quarantine, which are discarded. The next section shows that governance cycle in detail.
sec.lifecycle.intro: Not every lesson becomes a rule. Not every lesson is discarded. Reflect and auto-learn produce candidates. The lifecycle separates the wheat from the chaff with a simple rule: confidence routing. High confidence graduates directly, medium enters the queue, low disappears. And every graduate needs a rationale, no exceptions.
sec.lifecycle.routing.title: Confidence routing in one decision
sec.lifecycle.routing.high.desc: rule confirmed by clear evidence · bug fixed, test passing, decision validated
sec.lifecycle.routing.medium.desc: pattern observed once or twice · needs more cases to confirm
sec.lifecycle.routing.low.desc: guess, intuition without evidence, session-specific detail
sec.lifecycle.tools.title: The 8 brain scripts (Python stdlib only)
sec.lifecycle.tools.row.learn: immediate graduate · stages and graduates in one step
sec.lifecycle.tools.row.recall: looks up graduated lessons before risky work
sec.lifecycle.tools.row.reject: rejects with a reason · preserves history in rejected/
sec.lifecycle.tools.row.list: lists pending items ordered by priority
sec.lifecycle.tools.row.show: dashboard · grad count, pending, last grad
sec.lifecycle.terminal.list.title: $ list_candidates.py
sec.lifecycle.terminal.recall.title: $ recall.py "add created_at column"
sec.lifecycle.daemon.title: Auto-learn · the daemon that studies while you work
sec.lifecycle.daemon.desc: launchd fires dev.brain.auto-learn every 30 minutes. The daemon scans git activity, calls local Gemma 4 (Ollama, localhost:11434), extracts 0 to 3 lessons and routes them. If Ollama isn't running, it fails silently. Never blocks your commit.
sec.lifecycle.steward.title: Periodic curation · the memory-steward
sec.lifecycle.steward.desc: Automatic capture is only half the cycle. Without periodic review, memory accumulates duplicates with different wording, obsolete lessons nobody rejects, and contradictions the rerank tries to reconcile and fails. The memory-steward is a manual agent you invoke from time to time to do that cleanup. It doesn't write new memory; it audits what's already there.
sec.lifecycle.steward.step1.title: 1 · Audit
sec.lifecycle.steward.step1.desc: List pending candidates via list_candidates.py. Run show.py to see the dashboard of stale and graduated by age.
sec.lifecycle.steward.step2.title: 2 · Flag
sec.lifecycle.steward.step2.desc: Spot duplicates (qmd query on the claim), contradicting lessons (same area, opposite decisions) and stale memory (last_updated > 90 days and zero recall hits).
sec.lifecycle.steward.step3.title: 3 · Decide
sec.lifecycle.steward.step3.desc: graduate.py with rationale to promote, reject.py --reason to archive a duplicate, manual edit to realign a contradicting context. Reindex at the end.
sec.lifecycle.steward.cadence: Suggested cadence: run it when recall.py starts returning contradicting matches, or weekly on active projects. The clearest signal is when the agent searches and cites two lessons that contradict each other. That's memory asking for help.
sec.lifecycle.roles.skill.label: Skill
sec.lifecycle.roles.skill.desc: fires at session end · captures 1 to 3 lessons from the current session
sec.lifecycle.roles.daemon.label: Daemon
sec.lifecycle.roles.daemon.desc: runs every 30min · scans git activity between sessions
sec.lifecycle.roles.skill2.label: Skill on-demand
sec.lifecycle.roles.skill2.desc: you ask explicitly · extracts knowledge from the whole conversation
sec.lifecycle.roles.agent.label: Agent (manual)
sec.lifecycle.roles.agent.desc: periodic curation · audits stale, flags contradictions
sec.lifecycle.rationale.title: --rationale is not rubber-stamp
sec.lifecycle.rationale.desc: "Evidence holds" is not a rationale. "PR #234 fixes the bug, test in tests/auth/test_refresh.py passes" is. The system demands free text precisely to force citable evidence. Whoever graduates today has to explain it to whoever consults it tomorrow.
sec.anti-patterns.intro: Living memory is fragile. Five mistakes make the system degrade in weeks. Four are easy to fall into without noticing. Knowing each one saves months of search giving the wrong answer.
sec.anti-patterns.ap1.bad.title: 1. Duplicate memory without searching first
sec.anti-patterns.ap1.bad.desc: You create project/timestamps.md without running qmd search. A month later, somebody created project/date-conventions.md. Both say slightly different things.
sec.anti-patterns.ap1.bad.consequence: Consequence: rerank gets confused. Top-3 comes back with contradicting chunks. The AI answers badly.
sec.anti-patterns.ap1.good.title: Query first, write second
sec.anti-patterns.ap1.good.desc: Before creating a new file, always qmd query agent-memory "<topic>". If something already exists, update what's there.
sec.anti-patterns.ap1.good.action: Reuse > create. Update last_updated. Keep one file per domain.
sec.anti-patterns.ap2.bad.title: 2. Prose-heavy memory
sec.anti-patterns.ap2.bad.desc: You write paragraphs telling the decision's story. Looks pretty for humans, terrible for the chunker. The boundary lands mid-sentence, the embedding gets diluted.
sec.anti-patterns.ap2.bad.consequence: Consequence: semantic search fails. The retrieved chunk doesn't answer the question on its own.
sec.anti-patterns.ap2.good.title: 1 bullet = 1 fact
sec.anti-patterns.ap2.good.desc: Each bullet has to make sense on its own. No "this", "that", "as I said above". Each line is a potential search.
sec.anti-patterns.ap2.good.action: Mental test: if this bullet showed up alone in a search, does it make sense? If not, rewrite.
sec.anti-patterns.ap3.bad.title: 3. Graduate without evidence
sec.anti-patterns.ap3.bad.desc: Auto-learn queues 8 candidates. You graduate all of them with --rationale "evidence holds". A month later, the AI recalls false lessons that have become rules.
sec.anti-patterns.ap3.bad.consequence: Consequence: the system trusts distilled hallucinations. Every mistake becomes law.
sec.anti-patterns.ap3.good.title: Rationale with auditable link
sec.anti-patterns.ap3.good.desc: Every graduate cites a PR, commit, or test file. "PR #234, tests/auth/test_refresh.py passes". If you can't cite, reject or stage.
sec.anti-patterns.ap3.good.action: Accepting 2 lessons a week with strong rationale beats 20 with "evidence holds".
sec.anti-patterns.ap4.bad.title: 4. Lock in a tool directory
sec.anti-patterns.ap4.bad.desc: Memory lives in .claude/memory/. When the team opens Cursor or Codex, nobody finds it. Each tool recreates its own version. Triples the work.
sec.anti-patterns.ap4.bad.consequence: Consequence: knowledge trapped by tool choice. Switching cost becomes prohibitive.
sec.anti-patterns.ap4.good.title: Tool-agnostic in .agents/
sec.anti-patterns.ap4.good.desc: Memory in .agents/memory/. .claude/REDIRECT.md, .cursor/rules/project.mdc and the Codex MCP all point there.
sec.anti-patterns.ap4.good.action: Migrate tools without losing a line. The knowledge belongs to the project, not the editor.
sec.anti-patterns.ap5.bad.title: 5. Reflect without dedup
sec.anti-patterns.ap5.bad.desc: Reflect runs without checking recent reflections. In two weeks you have 5 reflection files saying "refresh tokens must rotate" with slightly different wording.
sec.anti-patterns.ap5.bad.consequence: Consequence: memory bloat. Rerank degrades because embeddings get saturated with variants.
sec.anti-patterns.ap5.good.title: Query before writing a reflection
sec.anti-patterns.ap5.good.desc: Reflect reads a short git log and runs qmd query on the topic. If a lesson already covers it, update last_updated instead of creating a new file.
sec.anti-patterns.ap5.good.action: New reflection only when the topic is new. Refinement updates the existing one.
sec.anti-patterns.ap6.bad.title: 6. Memory without periodic curation
sec.anti-patterns.ap6.bad.desc: Reflect and auto-learn write, nobody reviews. In three months memory has duplicates with different wording, obsolete lessons that contradict new decisions, and stale files nobody consults anymore. The agent searches, gets conflicting matches, and the rerank tries to reconcile what cannot be reconciled.
sec.anti-patterns.ap6.bad.consequence: Consequence: retrieval quality degrades silently. You only notice when the agent cites an obsolete lesson as current truth.
sec.anti-patterns.ap6.good.title: Memory-steward on a fixed cadence
sec.anti-patterns.ap6.good.desc: Run the memory-steward weekly, or when recall.py returns contradicting matches. Short cycle: list_candidates → flag duplicates via qmd query → reject.py with --reason or graduate.py with --rationale → reindex. Thirty minutes every two weeks avoids debt that takes days to clean up.
sec.anti-patterns.ap6.good.action: Curation isn't overhead. It's what separates living memory from a graveyard of notes.
sec.anti-patterns.summary.title: Pattern across the six errors: saving on human judgment
sec.anti-patterns.summary.desc: All six come from the same root: wanting to skip the review step. Duplication happens when you don't search. Prose happens when you don't edit. Graduate without evidence happens when you just glance. Tool lock happens when you don't think about portability. Reflect without dedup happens when you trust automation 100%. Memory without curation happens when you confuse "automate capture" with "automate judgment". Living memory only lives because the human still decides.
sec.proximos-passos.intro: Four steps to move from static memory to living memory this week. Each step is independent, each one already delivers value.
sec.proximos-passos.step.1.title: Install QMD
sec.proximos-passos.step.1.desc: Follow the official setup at github.com/tobi/qmd. The repo handles model downloads (embeddinggemma and reranker) on the first run. Confirm with qmd --version.
sec.proximos-passos.step.1.note: The first run may take a few minutes to download the models. From the second on it's instant.
sec.proximos-passos.step.2.title: Create the first memory
sec.proximos-passos.step.2.desc: Move what's already in .claude/memory/ or .cursor/rules/ to .agents/memory/project/. Use YAML frontmatter + dense bullets. Start with 1 or 2 files. No need to migrate everything at once.
sec.proximos-passos.step.3.title: Index and test the search
sec.proximos-passos.step.3.desc: Register the collection, generate the embeddings, run the first query. If top-1 returns the relevant chunk, you're ready. If something weird comes back, review the .md format.
sec.proximos-passos.step.3.note: For future reindexing, pick one of two: qmd embed agent-memory --force directly, or create a wrapper .agents/engine/bin/qmd-reindex.sh that also runs your flow's triggers.
sec.proximos-passos.step.4.title: Enable reflect at the end of sessions
sec.proximos-passos.step.4.desc: Create the reflect skill at .agents/skills/reflect/SKILL.md with the 5-step protocol. Run it manually at the end of the first sessions to calibrate. Then automate via post-commit hook or daemon.
sec.proximos-passos.step.4.note: Start manual. Once you're comfortable with the reflection file format, add the auto-learn daemon (Ollama + Gemma 4) to capture between sessions.
sec.proximos-passos.bonus.title: Agent Brain OS · Interactive Blueprint
sec.proximos-passos.bonus.desc: The entire architecture of this lesson in a single clickable diagram. Trigger each of the four flows (auto-learn, reflect, conversation-digest, context-retrieval) and watch data packets traverse the system in real time, with CLI and config snippets explaining what's happening.
sec.proximos-passos.mantra.label: Part 11 Mantra:
sec.proximos-passos.mantra.text: "AI doesn't need to remember everything. It needs to remember what matters, and you need to decide what matters."
sec.problema.pre.load: # Agent startup (load-all) [load] .claude/memory/MEMORY.md → 320 tokens [load] .claude/memory/user_role.md → 180 tokens [load] .claude/memory/feedback_tests.md → 640 tokens [load] .claude/memory/feedback_db.md → 820 tokens [load] .claude/memory/project_auth.md → 1,240 tokens [load] .claude/memory/project_api.md → 1,560 tokens [load] .claude/memory/reference_jira.md → 410 tokens ... 193 more files ... TOTAL startup context: ~18,000 tokens # Question: "what's the timestamp convention?" # The AI has already seen everything. It answers. But it paid the price of loading it all, # including 199 files irrelevant to this question.
sec.problema.pre.rag: # Agent startup (RAG) [load] .agents/AGENTS.md → 480 tokens [load] .agents/state/STATE.md → 220 tokens TOTAL startup context: ~700 tokens # Question: "what's the timestamp convention?" [query] qmd query agent-memory "timestamp utc convention" → top-3 chunks returned: 1. project/timestamps.md rank 0.94 ~180 tokens 2. project/api-spec.md rank 0.71 ~120 tokens 3. reflections/2026-04-01 rank 0.65 ~90 tokens # Final context used: 700 + 390 = ~1,090 tokens # 94% lighter. And only loaded what was relevant.
sec.qmd.pre.storage: ~/.cache/qmd/index.sqlite # Everything in a single file. # 10MB for a small project. # 30 to 50MB for large repos with docs + code. # Backup is cp. Reset is rm.
sec.qmd.pre.setup: # Register the memory folder as a collection qmd collection add agent-memory ~/.agents/memory --mask "**/*.md" # Generate embeddings (once, then incremental) qmd embed agent-memory # Manual reindex (when you edit .md outside the normal flow) qmd embed agent-memory --force
sec.qmd.pre.search: # Pure lexical search (fast, for exact terms) qmd search agent-memory "timestamp utc" # Pure vector search (semantic) qmd vsearch agent-memory "how to serialize dates" # Hybrid search + rerank (the recommended path) qmd query agent-memory "date convention in the project" --top 5 # Structured JSON for agent consumption qmd query agent-memory "auth refresh flow" --json -n 3
sec.qmd.pre.mcp: # Start QMD as an MCP server (ties into Session 5) qmd mcp --http --port 8080 # The agent talks via MCP, exposes tools: # query · hybrid search # get · fetch document by path # multi_get · fetch multiple paths # status · index health # In .claude/settings.json or .cursor/mcp: { "mcpServers": { "qmd": { "transport": "http", "url": "http://localhost:8080" } } }
sec.memoria-estatica.pre.agnostic: # Each tool points to .agents/, not to its own folder Claude Code ──► CLAUDE.md ──► .agents/AGENTS.md Cursor ──► .cursor/rules/... ──► .agents/AGENTS.md Codex / Oz ──► qmd MCP + skills ──► .agents/ # .claude/ becomes just a redirector with 3 files: # settings.json, settings.local.json, REDIRECT.md
sec.memoria-estatica.pre.tree: .agents/ ├─ AGENTS.md # master index · ~500 tokens · always read ├─ memory/ │ ├─ project/ # domain knowledge │ │ ├─ auth-flow.md │ │ ├─ timestamps.md │ │ └─ api-contracts.md │ ├─ brain/ # AI infrastructure knowledge │ ├─ lessons/ │ │ ├─ lessons.jsonl # source of truth (structured) │ │ ├─ LESSONS.md # human view (auto-generated) │ │ ├─ candidates/ # pending review │ │ └─ rejected/ # decision history │ └─ reflections/ # 1 file per meaningful session ├─ state/ │ ├─ STATE.md # what's being worked on right now │ └─ pending-analysis.md # produced by the post-commit hook └─ engine/ # the qmd engine
sec.memoria-estatica.pre.prose-bad:
In our project we usually
store timestamps in UTC because
it avoids timezone problems
when we deploy across
different regions. I think this
was decided at some point but
I don't remember exactly when.
sec.limite-estatico.pre.terminal: # You spent 6 months curating .cursor/rules/... $ cd project-x $ codex → Codex doesn't read .cursor/rules → Your curated rules become dead context → You rewrite them in Codex's format → 6 months later, you switch to Claude Code → Rewrite again
sec.lifecycle.pre.list: ID: c-2026-05-18-001 priority: high staged: 2 days ago · source: reflect claim: "Always serialize timestamps as UTC ISO-8601 at the API boundary" conditions: [api, serialization] ID: c-2026-05-17-003 priority: medium staged: 3 days ago · source: auto-learn claim: "Prefer connection pooling for FastAPI DB access" conditions: [fastapi, db] ID: c-2026-05-15-007 priority: low staged: 5 days ago · source: auto-learn claim: "Reuse Pydantic schema between handlers" conditions: [pydantic, refactor]
sec.lifecycle.pre.recall: # Top matches across graduated lessons [Match 0.82] "Always serialize timestamps as UTC ISO-8601 at the API boundary" graduated: 2026-05-18 conditions: [api, serialization] [Match 0.71] "Migration columns added in transactions, never inline" graduated: 2026-04-22 conditions: [migration, db] [Match 0.58] "All DB timestamps are TIMESTAMPTZ in PostgreSQL" graduated: 2026-03-10 conditions: [postgres, schema] # 3 applicable lessons. Read before the work.
sec.lifecycle.pre.daemon: # Manual: dry-run scan of the last 30 minutes $ python3 .agents/tools/auto-learn.py --dry-run # Force: ignore last-run, scan 4h back $ python3 .agents/tools/auto-learn.py --force # Real-time log $ tail -f .agents/state/auto-learn.log
sec.lifecycle.pre.steward: # Typical curation cycle (weekly or when recall gets noisy) $ python3 .agents/tools/brain/list_candidates.py --priority high $ python3 .agents/tools/brain/show.py --stale-days 90 # Reject an identified duplicate $ python3 .agents/tools/brain/reject.py L-2026-04-12-a \ --reason "Duplicate of L-2026-03-08-c, same claim about rate limits" # Promote a validated candidate $ python3 .agents/tools/brain/graduate.py L-2026-05-01-b \ --rationale "Confirmed in PR #312 and tests/auth/test_refresh.py" # Reindex so qmd reflects the changes $ .agents/engine/bin/qmd-reindex.sh
sec.proximos-passos.pre.step4: mkdir -p .agents/skills/reflect mkdir -p .agents/memory/reflections mkdir -p .agents/tools/brain # add learn.py, stage.py, recall.py, graduate.py # from the .agents/AGENTS.md documentation
